item: Illustration Set: Hero / Empty State / Error / Onboarding / Achievement
definition_origin: An app illustration set is the curated bank of original artwork that fills the non-data surfaces — marketing hero panels, empty states (no items yet), error states (404, network failure, permission denied), onboarding screens (3-5 swipeable intros), and achievement/celebration moments (badges, milestones, confetti). The format originates in print editorial illustration (1950s magazine covers) and migrated into web design via Dropbox's hand-drawn UI illustrations (~2012), Slack's onboarding vignettes, and the modular character libraries that emerged 2018-2020 (unDraw, Open Doodles, Storyset, Humaaans). The 2026 reality: those free libraries became so over-used they now ARE the AI-slop signature in disguise.
core_purpose: **Visceral**: in moments where data is absent (empty), broken (error), or transitional (onboarding), the illustration carries the entire emotional load of the screen. A blank list says 'this app is broken'; the same list with an inviting empty-state illustration says 'this app is calm and intentional.' **Behavioral**: empty states should be invitational not embarrassing — instead of 'No items found' the illustration plus copy says 'Tap + to create your first habit.' Error states should be empathic not blame-the-user — the illustration shows 'we know this is frustrating, here's the recovery path.' Onboarding illustrations compress complex value props into single-glance metaphors. Achievement art delivers the dopamine hit that makes a habit-loop feel rewarding. **Reflective**: the illustration set is what differentiates a generic SaaS dashboard from a memorable product. Six months later, users remember the achievement confetti, not the form. For our user's Magician/Creator + Jester/Outlaw + Caregiver mix: illustrations are where archetype gets expressed at full volume — Caregiver in empty states (warm welcome, not scolding), Jester in error states (self-deprecating humor), Magician/Creator in onboarding (transformation promise), Hero in achievement (you-vs-the-world triumph).
when_needed: **Mandatory** for consumer mobile (every screen has at least one of empty/error/onboarding) and marketing landing pages (hero illustration is the single most-clicked-through visual). **Recommended** for B2B/dev-tool — even Linear, Vercel, and Stripe have custom illustrations for empty states, just in their own minimal restrained style. **Often skipped** in pure AI-wrapper apps that lean on conversational UI; even then, an empty conversation state benefits from one illustration. Surface checklist: marketing hero (1), category-page heroes (2-3), every list/feed empty state (3-5 unique), error states (404, network, permission, payment, generic) (5), onboarding (3-5), achievement/celebration (3-10 if gamified). Total minimum bank: ~15-25 unique illustrations for a full app.
format_options: **SVG** is the dominant format — resolution-independent, small file (5-30KB after SVGO), recolorable via CSS variables for light/dark mode. Limitation: complex shading and texture become bloated SVG files. **PNG/WebP** for textured/painterly illustrations where SVG balloons; PNG with alpha at 2x density (e.g., 800x600 PNG for a 400x300 display) — convert to WebP for 30-50% smaller files. **Lottie** for hero illustrations with subtle ambient animation (drifting clouds, blinking eyes, parallax) — 50-200KB. **Rive** for interactive achievement moments (state-machine driven confetti that responds to score) — 5-30KB. **Animated WebP / APNG** as fallback when Lottie is overkill — for short loops under 1 second. **3D glTF** rare and overkill for solo dev. Pros/cons: SVG = best for crisp geometric/flat illustrations, recolorable; PNG = best for painterly/textured looks but baked-in resolution; Lottie = beautiful subtle motion but author-time cost (After Effects); Rive = small + interactive but newer ecosystem and learning curve; WebP = smallest raster but poor support in some legacy contexts (mostly fine in 2026). Recommendation: SVG for empty/error/onboarding; PNG/WebP for hero if painterly; Lottie for achievement celebrations only.
file_size_performance: SVG: target 5-30KB after SVGO. PNG @ 2x: 50-200KB at 800x600. WebP: 30-100KB same resolution. Lottie: aim under 100KB, hard cap 200KB for hero. Total illustration bundle: keep under 1.5MB for a 20-illustration set after compression. Lazy-load non-critical illustrations (anything not on the first 3 screens of onboarding).
free_tools: **Free libraries (use with EXTREME CAUTION — see anti-slop)**: 1. **unDraw** (https://undraw.co) — free SVG, recolorable on site, no attribution required. **Slop signature warning**: the 'unDraw look' (long-bodied figures with single-color clothing, geometric backgrounds) is now instantly recognizable as 'I didn't pay for illustrations.' 2. **Storyset by Freepik** — five style families (Rafiki, Bro, Amico, Pana, Cuate); free with attribution unless you upgrade. Same slop-signature risk. 3. **Humaaans by Pablo Stanley** — modular human characters; iconic-but-overused look. 4. **Open Doodles** — Pablo Stanley's hand-drawn alternative, less overused than Humaaans, more Caregiver-archetype-friendly. 5. **Open Peeps** — another Pablo Stanley library. 6. **IRA Design** — geometric illustration kit. 7. **DrawKit** — free portion. **Free creation tools**: 8. **Figma Free** — vector authoring, the only realistic path to non-slop original illustrations. 9. **Inkscape** — free desktop alternative. 10. **Procreate** ($12.99 one-time, iPad) — best for hand-drawn texture (cheapest non-slop path). 11. **Krita** (FREE, desktop) — full painting app. **Free AI for illustration**: 12. **Recraft (FREE 50 cred/day)** — native SVG output, the highest-leverage free AI for illustration. 13. **Ideogram (25 imgs/day FREE)** — best free for text-in-image (achievement badges with words). 14. **Leonardo AI (150 tokens/day FREE)** — strong illustration models. 15. **Microsoft Designer (15 boosts/day FREE)** — DALL-E 3, decent illustration quality. 16. **Krea AI free tier** — real-time canvas. 17. **Google Imagen 4 in Gemini Pro** — already paid by user, primary tool.
paid_tools: **Starter ($10-15/mo)**: Recraft Basic ($12/mo, 1500 credits, full SVG export, no watermark) — single most useful upgrade for non-designer doing original illustrations. Storyset paid ($7/mo) only if you must use the Storyset look without attribution. **Pro ($20-30/mo)**: Adobe Firefly ($22.99/mo) for IP-indemnified output if shipping for B2B contract. Procreate one-time $12.99 + Apple Pencil + iPad for hand-drawn workflow. Affinity Designer ($89.99 one-time, no subscription) — actual professional vector tool, lifetime license, alternative to Adobe Illustrator. **Enterprise / one-shot**: hire a freelance illustrator for the brand-defining hero (Dribbble: $300-1500). For our user: stay free + Gemini Pro for now; if app gets PMF, single $500-1000 commission for the hero illustration is highest-ROI spend.
gemini_pro_workflow:
**Step 1 — Style guide writing with Gemini 2.5 Pro**: 'I need an illustration style guide for a {category} app, archetype {Magician/Creator + Caregiver}. List 8 visual constraints (e.g., line weight, color palette, character proportions, background treatment, perspective convention) that will keep 20+ illustrations feeling like one set. Avoid: unDraw look, Humaaans look, generic 3D blob look, gradient mesh, isometric voxel.'

**Step 2 — Imagen 4 hero illustration**: Prompt template: 'Illustration for {app concept}, flat 2D style with subtle hand-drawn line weight variation, color palette {3-4 hex codes}, single focal character or scene, balanced negative space, {perspective: front-facing / 3-quarter / overhead}, {mood adjective}, no gradient mesh, no 3D render, no isometric voxel, no AI-default purple-pastel, magazine editorial illustration quality.' Generate 30+ for one slot, cull to 1.

**Step 3 — Whisk for style transfer (until 2026-04-30; afterward Flow)**: Find a real reference illustrator on Dribbble/Are.na (e.g., Yukai Du, Tracie Ching, Owen Davey) — use their image as Style input, your composition as Subject. This is the single most powerful Gemini move for non-slop illustration.

**Step 4 — Imagen 4 SET generation with locked style**: Once you have a strong hero, use it as reference for the next 19 illustrations: 'Same illustration style as reference image, now showing {empty state / 404 / onboarding step 2}.'

**Step 5 — Build a Gem ('Illustration Set Generator')**: System prompt: 'You produce illustrations in the style of {locked references}, color palette {hex}, line weight {value}, character proportions {description}. Always respond with image generation. Never use: gradient mesh, 3D render, isometric voxel, AI-default pastel.' Pin the locked reference images.

**Step 6 — Veo for hero ambient loop**: Generate 4-second loop on the hero ('camera slowly pushes in', 'character blinks once'). Use as reference for Lottie author or as final asset if quality is acceptable.

**Step 7 — NotebookLM**: Drop in the 20 generated illustrations + your style guide. Ask 'identify visual inconsistencies — line weight, color, character proportions, perspective.' Catches drift across the set.
workflow_steps: 1. **Inventory the surfaces (30 min)**: List every screen needing illustration — 15-25 slots typical. Group by emotional tone (welcome, frustration, celebration). 2. **Style guide writing (60 min)**: Lock 8 visual constraints (line weight, palette, perspective, character proportions, level of detail, hand-drawn texture y/n, geometric vs organic, background treatment). 3. **Reference board (60 min)**: Mobbin + Are.na + Dribbble — 30 illustrations you envy with notes 'why it works.' 4. **Hero illustration first (4-6 hours)**: This is the canonical reference; everything else copies its style. 5. **AI generation pass (3-5 hours)**: Imagen 4 + Whisk for the remaining 19, locked to hero style. 6. **Vectorize / refine in Figma (8-15 hours, batched)**: Don't ship raw AI; trace or significantly clean up each one. 7. **Color tokenize (60 min)**: Replace all colors with brand tokens so light/dark mode swap is automatic. 8. **Export at correct size**: SVG for vector, PNG @ 2x for raster, run through SVGO and ImageOptim. 9. **Drop into app via shared component**: `<EmptyStateIllustration kind='no-habits'/>` resolves to the right asset. 10. **Audit pass (30 min)**: print all 20 in a grid; do they feel like one set? Kill outliers. **Total**: 25-40 hours for a full set first time; 5-8 hours for additions.
ai_prompt_templates:
**Template 1 — Hero illustration (Caregiver)**: 'Editorial illustration for a {app category} app landing page hero, single warm scene of {scene description}, flat 2D style with subtle hand-drawn line weight variation, color palette: {primary hex}, {secondary hex}, {accent hex}, soft cream background, balanced negative space, character has friendly oversized features, mood is calm-and-inviting, illustration quality of {Tracie Ching / Yukai Du / Owen Davey}, no gradient mesh, no isometric voxel, no 3D render, no unDraw style, no Humaaans style, 16:9 composition.'

**Template 2 — Empty state (invitational)**: 'Small spot illustration, single object or character {object: empty basket / sleeping cat / waiting balloon}, flat vector style matching style guide reference, {2 colors max} on white, gentle implication that something will arrive (visual hook to action), 600x400, no text in illustration.'

**Template 3 — Error 404 (empathic + slight humor)**: 'Spot illustration, character {looking at empty map / lost in maze / holding broken telescope}, flat vector style, mood is not-your-fault-and-it''s-fixable, {primary color} dominant, single soft accent of {warm hex}, no skull-and-crossbones cliché, no melted feature, 600x600, transparent.'

**Template 4 — Onboarding step**: 'Spot illustration of {core value prop scene} — {description with metaphor}, same flat vector style as hero reference, character or scene communicates {transformation promise / habit forming / community / privacy}, no on-image text, 800x600.'

**Template 5 — Achievement celebration**: 'Celebratory illustration of {character/object} surrounded by simple geometric confetti, color palette {hex tokens}, character expression is delighted, posture is arms raised, flat vector, dynamic but not chaotic, no AI-default sparkle overlay, 600x600, transparent background.'
claude_code_integration: Build `apps/mobile/src/components/Illustration.tsx` — a single component that maps slot enum (`'hero' | 'empty-habits' | 'error-network' | 'onboarding-1' | ... | 'achievement-streak-7'`) to imported asset. Use `react-native-svg` for SVG, `expo-image` for PNG/WebP, `lottie-react-native` for Lottie. Centralize asset paths so swapping illustrations is one file edit. For dark mode: SVG with `currentColor` + CSS-style theme tokens, OR ship two PNGs and pick via `useColorScheme()`. Add a Storybook screen `apps/mobile/.storybook/stories/Illustrations.stories.tsx` to review entire set on one screen — catches inconsistency drift instantly. Optimization script: `scripts/optimize-illustrations.sh` running `svgo` + `cwebp -q 85` recursively. Lazy-load: only `hero` and `onboarding-1` should be in the initial bundle; everything else loaded on-demand. For web: use Next.js `<Image priority={false}>` + dynamic imports of SVG-as-React-component. Claude Code prompt: 'add a new achievement illustration for streak-30, place SVG at assets/illustrations/achievement-streak-30.svg, add to Illustration enum, add Storybook story.'
emotional_design_integration: **Magician**: illustrations should hint at hidden depths — a character looking up at a starry pattern, a single key in a doorway with light beyond, transformation moments (chrysalis, sunrise). **Creator**: tools, paint splatter, half-finished work-in-progress, generative grids being assembled. **Jester**: characters making faces, pratfall poses, self-aware fourth-wall breaks (a character looking annoyed at the user's bug). **Outlaw**: leather/grit/desert palette, sharp angles, lone-figure silhouettes. **Hero**: chevron compositions, upward motion, spotlight effect, cape/wind. **Caregiver**: round shapes, warm low-saturation palette (peach + sage + cream), soft eyes, hugging gestures, cozy domestic settings. **Innocent**: round, clean, minimal, baby-soft palette, sun-and-cloud iconography. **Lover**: warm pinks, soft focus, intimate two-figure compositions. **Sage**: books, glasses, owl iconography, sepia-tinted, muted palette. For our user's archetype mix: Caregiver palette (warm) + Magician details (one uncanny element per piece — a glowing star, an unexplained third entity, a slightly impossible perspective) + Jester expressions on characters = unique archetype fingerprint nobody else has.
specific_examples: 1. **Headspace** (https://headspace.com) — Caregiver archetype illustrations by Andy Puddicombe's team; warm palette, hand-drawn wobble, simple shapes; the 'wobble' is anti-AI-slop by construction. 2. **Slack onboarding 2018-era** — vignettes by Pablo Stanley's team showing collaboration moments; iconic but now overused (the Slack illustration look spawned a thousand SaaS knockoffs). 3. **Duolingo achievement screens** — the streak-restoration screen with Duo crying is a masterclass in emotional design; archetype tension (Caregiver concern + Jester hyperbole) in one frame. 4. **Stripe Press / Stripe Docs illustrations** — restrained editorial, single muted color, classical engraving feel; the anti-blob aesthetic. 5. **Linear changelog illustrations** (https://linear.app/changelog) — flat geometric, single accent color, monochromatic backgrounds; archetype = Magician/Sage; minimum slop because they hand-craft each. 6. **Notion empty states** — the 'first-time' empty states with single mascot character pointing to action. 7. **Things 3 onboarding** (Cultured Code) — hand-drawn warmth, the opposite of AI-slop. 8. **Mailchimp marketing illustrations** — Jester archetype, weird-by-design (anthropomorphic hands, surrealist flourishes); the brand differentiator. 9. **Apple Memoji/Sticker system** — modular character library done right (vs Humaaans done overused). 10. **Are.na 'Editorial illustration' channel** — best free reference board for non-slop work. **References for style transfer Whisk inputs**: Yukai Du, Tracie Ching, Owen Davey, Malika Favre, Christoph Niemann.
anti_ai_slop_traps: **Tells specific to AI-generated illustrations**: (a) Gradient mesh blob backdrop — the purple-cyan smoothie behind every AI illustration; instant signature. (b) Generic isometric voxel scene — Cubic 30-degree-angle blocks with floating UI panels; the 'crypto whitepaper aesthetic.' (c) Six-fingered or melted hands when characters are present. (d) Plastic 3D pill characters — round Pixar-blender bodies with too-shiny skin. (e) Pastel mauve + minty teal palette default. (f) Floating UI cards in space without context. (g) Symmetrical-but-uncanny composition. (h) Generic 'diverse-stock-photo' character lineup that all share suspicious facial similarities. (i) The 'AI-default 3/4 view' for everything. (j) Tiny stars, sparkles, and floating particles added to convey 'magic' (the AI's go-to visual). **And the FREE-LIBRARY slop signatures**: (k) **unDraw look** — long-bodied figures, single-color clothing, single accent color background pattern; ANY designer recognizes it within 1 second. (l) **Humaaans look** — modular puzzle-piece characters with disconnected limb seams. (m) **Storyset Bro/Pana/Amico** — all five Storyset families have telltale silhouettes. **Counter-prompts**: 'editorial illustration', 'hand-drawn line weight variation', 'no gradient mesh', 'no isometric voxel', 'no 3D render', '{specific illustrator name reference}', 'flat vector with subtle texture'. **Counter-process**: ALWAYS introduce hand-drawn variability — open in Figma, manually wobble lines slightly off-grid, vary stroke weight, introduce one intentional asymmetry. The AI can't help being too clean; your job is to break the cleanness with intent. Best move: pick one obscure illustrator on Are.na/Behance, use their work as Whisk Style input, get something the AI training set hasn't memorized.
common_pitfalls: Mixing illustration styles — 5 unDraw + 5 Storyset + 5 custom = visual chaos that screams 'unfinished design.' Skipping dark-mode variants — bright-white-background illustrations destroy dark UI. Overusing the hero illustration in micro contexts — a hero at 50px tall is unreadable. Underestimating SVG file size for complex illustrations — a Storyset SVG can hit 200-500KB if not optimized. License lapses — many free libraries require attribution; missing it can be a contract violation. Cultural insensitivity — character skin tones, gender expression, ability representation; double-check before shipping. Accessibility forgotten — illustration without alt text is invisible to screen readers. Achievement illustrations triggering for users who don't care — gamification on the wrong audience feels infantilizing (especially in B2B). Performance — Lottie autoplay on first paint blocks LCP. Over-relying on AI — the 'I generated 100 and shipped them' pipeline produces inconsistent set unless you enforce style transfer rigorously.
accessibility_notes: Every illustration needs a meaningful `alt` text on web (``) or `accessibilityLabel` on RN (``); for purely decorative illustrations use empty alt (`alt=''`) or `accessibilityElementsHidden={true}` so screen readers skip them. Color must not be the only signal — error illustrations should not rely on red alone, achievement illustrations should not rely on green alone. Provide `prefers-reduced-motion` static fallbacks for any animated Lottie/Rive (achievement confetti is the typical offender). Cognitive load: avoid putting illustrations in dense data screens. For users with photosensitive epilepsy: no flashing under 3Hz, especially in achievement celebration animations. WCAG 2.2 — meaningful illustrations should have text alternatives in adjacent UI, not embedded in the SVG.
mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile: SVG via react-native-svg, PNG/WebP via expo-image, Lottie via lottie-react-native. Bundle size matters — every KB ships in the APK/IPA. Lazy-load aggressively. Web: SVG inline as React component (best perf — no extra request) OR external SVG file with HTTP/2 multiplex. Lottie via lottie-web (~80KB lib + your animation). Web hero illustrations should support srcSet for responsive scaling. Mobile-only: respect Low Power Mode by disabling ambient animations. Web-only: the hero illustration is part of the OG share image — design for both fullscreen and 1200x630 crop.
